questions.question_text: If 4a + 2b + c = 0, then the equation 3ax 2 + 2bx + c = 0 has at least one real root lying in the interval.
questions.question_html: If 4a + 2b + c = 0, then the equation 3ax2 + 2bx + c = 0 has at least one real root lying in the interval.
questions.explanation: Let f(x) = ax 3 + bx 2 + cx + d ...(i) {tex}\therefore{/tex} f(0) = d f(2) = a(2) 3 + b(2) 2 + c(2) + d = 8a + 4b + 2c + d = 2(4a + 2b + c) + d {tex}\because{/tex} 4a + 2b + c = 0 {Given} = 2 (0) + d = 0 + d = d f is continuous in closed interval [0, 2] and f is derivable in the open interval (0, 2). Also, f(0) = f(2) As per Rolle’s Theorem, f’(α) = 0 for 0 < α < 2 f’(x) = 3ax 2 + 2bx + c {tex}\therefore{/tex} f’(α) = 3aα 2 + 2b(α) + c 3aα 2 + 2b(α) + c = 0 Hence equation (i) has at least one root in the interval (0, 2). Thus, f’(x) must have one root in the interval (0, 2).

questions.question_text: If {tex}A_{\alpha}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} \cos \alpha & \sin \alpha \\ -\sin \alpha & \cos \alpha \end{array}\right]{/tex} then {tex}\left(A_{\alpha}\right)^{2}{/tex} = ?

questions.explanation: Given, {tex}A_{\alpha}=\left(\begin{array}{cc} \cos \alpha & \sin \alpha \\ -\sin \alpha & \cos \alpha \end{array}\right){/tex} {tex}A_{a}^{2}=\left(\begin{array}{cc} \cos \alpha & \sin \alpha \\ -\sin \alpha & \cos \alpha \end{array}\right)\left(\begin{array}{cc} \cos \alpha & \sin \alpha \\ -\sin \alpha & \cos \alpha \end{array}\right){/tex} {tex}=\left(\begin{array}{cc} \cos \alpha \times \cos \alpha-\sin \alpha \times \sin \alpha & \cos \alpha \times \sin \alpha+\sin \alpha \times \cos \alpha \\ -\sin \alpha \times \cos \alpha-\cos \alpha \times \sin \alpha & -\sin \alpha \times \sin \alpha+\cos \alpha \times \cos \alpha \end{array}\right){/tex} {tex}=\left(\begin{array}{cc} \cos ^{2} \alpha-\sin ^{2} \alpha & \cos \alpha \sin \alpha+\sin \alpha \cos \alpha \\ -\sin \alpha \cos \alpha-\cos \alpha \sin \alpha & -\sin ^{2} \alpha+\cos ^{2} \alpha \end{array}\right){/tex} {tex}=\left(\begin{array}{cc} \cos 2 \alpha & \sin 2 \alpha \\ -\sin 2 \alpha & \cos 2 \alpha \end{array}\right){/tex}

questions.question_text: The point at which the maximum value of x + y, subject to the constraints x + 2y {tex}\le{/tex} 70, 2x + y {tex}\le{/tex} 95, x, y {tex}\ge{/tex} 0 is obtained, is
questions.question_html: The point at which the maximum value of x + y, subject to the constraints x + 2y {tex}\le{/tex} 70, 2x + y {tex}\le{/tex} 95, x, y {tex}\ge{/tex} 0 is obtained, is
questions.explanation: We need to maximize the function z = x + y Converting the given inequations into equations, we obtain x + 2y = 70, 2x + y = 95, x = 0 and y = 0 Region represented by x + 2y {tex}\leq{/tex} 70 : The line x + 2y = 70 meets the coordinate axes at A(70, 0) and B(0, 35) respectively. By joining these points we obtain the line x + 2y = 70. Clearly (0, 0) satisfies the inequation x + 2y {tex}\leq{/tex} 70 . So, the region containing the origin represents the solution set of the inequation x + 2y {tex}\leq{/tex} 70. Region represented by 2x + y {tex}\leq{/tex} 95 : The line 2x + y = 95 meets the coordinate axes at {tex}C\left(\frac{95}{2}, 0\right){/tex} respectively. By joining these points we obtain the line 2x + y = 95 Clearly (0, 0) satisfies the inequation 2x + y {tex}\leq{/tex} 95 . So, the region containing the origin represents the solution set of the inequation 2x + y {tex}\leq{/tex} 95 Region represented by x {tex}\geq{/tex} 0 and y {tex}\geq{/tex} 0 : since, every point in the first quadrant satisfies these inequations. So, the first quadrant is the region represented by the inequations x {tex}\geq{/tex} 0, and y {tex}\geq{/tex} 0 The feasible region determined by the system of constraints x + 2y {tex}\leq{/tex} 70, 2x + y {tex}\leq{/tex} 95, x {tex}\geq{/tex} 0, and y {tex}\geq{/tex} 0 are as follows. The corner points of the feasible region are O(0, 0), C( {tex}\frac{95}{2}{/tex} , 0) E(40, 15) and B(0, 35). The value fo Z at these corner points are as follows. Corner point : z = x + y O(0, 0) : 0 + 0 = 0 {tex}C\left(\frac{95}{2}, 0\right){/tex} : {tex}\frac{95}{2}+0=\frac{95}{2}{/tex} E(40, 15) : 40 + 15 = 55 B(0, 35) : 0 + 35 = 35 We see that maximum value of the objective function Z is 55 which is at (40, 15).

questions.question_text: There are two values of 'a' which make determinant, {tex}\Delta=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} {1} & {-2} & {5} \\ {2} & {a} & {-1} \\ {0} & {4} & {2 a} \end{array}\right|=86{/tex} , then sum of these numbers is
questions.question_html: There are two values of 'a' which make determinant, {tex}\Delta=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} {1} & {-2} & {5} \\ {2} & {a} & {-1} \\ {0} & {4} & {2 a} \end{array}\right|=86{/tex}, then sum of these numbers is
questions.explanation: Using determinant rule, we have {tex}\Delta = (2a^2 + 4) + 2(4a + 0)+5(8) = 86{/tex} {tex}= 2a^2 + 4 + 8a + 40 = 86\\ = 2a^2 + 8a - 42 = 0\\ =a^2 + 4a - 21 {/tex} But given that, {tex}\Delta{/tex} = 0 {tex}a = \frac{{ - 4 \pm \sqrt {16 - 4 \times - 21} }}{2}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{ - 4 \pm 10}}{2}{/tex} {tex}a = 3 ~~and ~-7{/tex} Therefore, Required Sum = -4.

questions.explanation: Given , f(x) = x + {tex}\frac{4}{x}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow f'(x)=1-\frac { 4 }{ { { x^{ 2 } } } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow f'(x)=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x=\pm 2{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow f''(x)=\frac { 8 }{ { { x^{ 3 } } } } {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow f''(2)=\frac { 8 }{ 8 } =1>0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow f''(-2)=\frac { 8 }{ { -8 } } =-1<0{/tex} So, f(x) has a local minima at x = 2 and a local minima at x= -2.

questions.question_text: Find the value of f(0), so that the function {tex}f(x)=\frac{(27-2 x)^{1 / 3}-3}{9-3(243+5 x)^{1 / 5}}(x \neq 0){/tex} is continuous, is given by
questions.question_html: Find the value of f(0), so that the function {tex}f(x)=\frac{(27-2 x)^{1 / 3}-3}{9-3(243+5 x)^{1 / 5}}(x \neq 0){/tex} is continuous, is given by
questions.explanation: For f(x) to be continuous at x = 0, we must have {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0} f(x)=f(0){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow f(0)=\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0} f(x)=\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{(27-2 x)^{\frac{1}{3}}-3}{9-3(243+5 x)^{\frac{1}{5}}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow f(0)=\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{(27-2 x)^{\frac{1}{3}}-27^{\frac{1}{3}}}{3\left(243^{\frac{1}{5}}-(243+5 x)^{\frac{1}{5}}\right)}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{3} \lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\frac{(27-2 x)^{\frac{1}{3}}-27^{\frac{1}{3}}}{x}}{\frac{\left(243^{\frac{1}{5}}-(243+5 x)^{\frac{1}{5}}\right)}{x}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{-1}{3} \lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\frac{(27-2 x)^{\frac{1}{3}}-27^{\frac{1}{3}}}{x}}{\frac{\left((243+5 x)^{\frac{1}{5}}-243^{\frac{1}{5}}\right)}{x}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2}{15} \lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\frac{(27-2 x)^{\frac{1}{3}}-27^{\frac{1}{3}}}{-2 x}}{\frac{\left((243+5 x)^{\frac{1}{5}}-243^{\frac{1}{5}}\right)}{5 x}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2}{15} \lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\frac{(27-2 x)^{\frac{1}{3}}-27^{\frac{1}{3}}}{27-2 x-27}}{\frac{\left((243+5 x)^{\frac{1}{5}}-243^{\frac{1}{5}}\right)}{243+5 x-243}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2}{15} \times \frac{\frac{1}{3} \times 27^{\frac{-2}{3}}}{\frac{1}{5} \times 243^{\frac{-4}{5}}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2}{15} \times \frac{\frac{1}{3} \times \frac{1}{27^{\frac{2}{3}}}}{\frac{1}{5} \times \frac{1}{243^{\frac{4}{5}}}}{/tex} = 2

questions.question_html: If y = tan-1 {tex}\left(\frac{1+x^{2}}{1-x^{2}}\right){/tex} then {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}={/tex} ?
questions.explanation: Given that {tex}y=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1+x^{2}}{1-x^{2}}\right){/tex} Let x 2 = tan {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \theta=\tan ^{-1} x^{2}{/tex} Hence, {tex}y=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1+\tan \theta}{1-\tan \theta}\right){/tex} Using {tex}\tan \left(\frac{\pi}{4}+x\right)=\frac{1+\tan x}{1-\tan x}{/tex} , we obtain {tex}y=\tan ^{-1} \tan \left(\frac{\pi}{4}+\theta\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{\pi}{4}+\theta=\frac{\pi}{4}+\tan ^{-1}\left(x^{2}\right){/tex} Differentiating with respect to x, we obtain {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{1+x^{4}} \times 2 x=\frac{2 x}{1+x^{4}}{/tex}

questions.question_text: The radius of the base of a cone is increasing at the rate of 3 cm/minute and the altitude is decreasing at the rate of 4 cm/minute. The rate of change of lateral surface when the radius = 7cm and altitude 24 cm is
questions.question_html: The radius of the base of a cone is increasing at the rate of 3 cm/minute and the altitude is decreasing at the rate of 4 cm/minute. The rate of change of lateral surface when the radius = 7cm and altitude 24 cm is
questions.explanation: Let r be the radius, h be the height and S be the lateral surface area of the cone at any time t. Given: {tex}\frac{d r}{d t}={/tex} 3cm/min and {tex}\frac{d h}{d t}=-4 c m / \min{/tex} Here, l 2 = h 2 + r 2 {tex}\Rightarrow l=\sqrt{(24)^{2}+(7)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow l=\sqrt{625}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} l = 25 {tex}S=\pi r l{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow S^{2}=(\pi r l)^{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow S^{2}=\pi^{2} r^{2}\left(h^{2}+r^{2}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow S^{2}=\pi^{2} r^{4}+\pi^{2} h^{2} r^{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 2 S \frac{d S}{d t}=4 \pi^{2} r^{3} \frac{d r}{d t}{/tex} {tex}+2 \pi^{2} r^{2} h \frac{d h}{d t}+2 \pi^{2} h^{2} r \frac{d r}{d t}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 2 \pi r l \frac{d S}{d t}=2 \pi^{2} r h{/tex} {tex}\left[\frac{2 r^{2}}{h} \frac{d r}{d t}+r \frac{d h}{d t}+h \frac{d r}{d t}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 25 \frac{d S}{d t}{/tex} {tex}=24 \pi\left[\frac{2(7)^{2}}{24} \times 3-7 \times 4+24 \times 3\right]{/tex} [Given: r = 7, h = 24] {tex}\Rightarrow 25 \frac{d S}{d t}=24 \pi\left[\frac{49}{4}-28+72\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 25 \frac{d S}{d t}=24 \pi\left[\frac{49+288-112}{4}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d S}{d t}=24 \pi\left[\frac{225}{100}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d S}{d t}=24 \pi(2.25){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d S}{d t}=54 \pi \mathrm{cm}^{2} / \mathrm{sec}{/tex}

questions.question_text: The function f(x) = 4 sin 3 x - 6 sin 2 x + 12 sinx + 100 is strictly
questions.question_html: The function f(x) = 4 sin3x - 6 sin2x + 12 sinx + 100 is strictly
questions.explanation: We have, f(x) = 4 sin 3 x - 6 sin 2 x + 12 sin x + 100 {tex}\therefore{/tex} f'(x) = 12 sin 2 x {tex}\cdot{/tex} cos x - 12 sin x {tex}\cdot{/tex} cos x + 12 cos x = 12 cos x [sin 2 x - sin x + 1] = 12 cos x [sin 2 x + (1 - sinx)] Now 1 - sin x {tex}\geq{/tex} 0 and sin 2 x {tex}\geq{/tex} 0 {tex}\therefore{/tex} sin 2 x + 1 - sin x > 0 Hence f'(x) > 0, when cos x > 0 i.e., {tex}x \in\left(\frac{-\pi}{2}, \frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} So, f(x) is increasing when {tex}x \in\left(-\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} and f'(x) < 0, when cos x < 0 i.e., {tex}x \in\left(\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{3 \pi}{2}\right){/tex} Hence, f(x) is decreasing when {tex}x \in\left(\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{3 \pi}{2}\right){/tex} Hence, f(x) is decreasing in {tex}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}, \pi\right){/tex}

questions.explanation: First, we will convert the given inequations into equations, we obtain the following equations: 3x + 4y = 24, 8x + 6y = 48, x = 5, y = 6, x = 0 and y = 0 The line 3x + 4y = 24 meets the coordinate axis at A(8, 0) and B(0, 6). Join these points to obtain the line 3x + 4y = 24 Clearly, (0, 0) satisfies the inequation 3x + 4y {tex}\leq{/tex} 24 . So, the region in x y -plane that contains the origin represents the solution set of the given equation. The line 8x + 6y = 48 meets the coordinate axis at C(6, 0) and D(0, 8) . Join these points to obtain the line 8x + 6y = 48 Clearly, (0, 0) satisfies the inequation 8x + 6y {tex}\leq{/tex} 48 . So, the region in x y -plane that contains the origin represents the solution set of the given equation. x = 5 is the line passing through x = 5 parallel to the Y axis. y = 6 is the line passing through y = 6 parallel to the X axis. The region represented by x {tex}\geq{/tex} 0 and y {tex}\geq{/tex} 0 : since, every point in the first quadrant satisfies these inequations. So, the first quadrant is the region represented by the inequations. These lines are drawn using a suitable scale. The corner points of the feasible region are O(0, 0), G(5, 0), {tex}F\left(5, \frac{4}{3}\right){/tex} , {tex}E\left(\frac{24}{7}, \frac{24}{7}\right){/tex} and B(0, 6) The values of Z at these corner points are as follows: Corner point : Z = 4x + 3y O(0, 0) : {tex}4 \times 0+3 \times 0=0{/tex} G(5, 0) : {tex}4 \times 5+3 \times 0=20{/tex} {tex}F\left(5, \frac{4}{3}\right){/tex} : {tex}4 \times 5+3 \times \frac{4}{3}=24{/tex} {tex}E\left(\frac{24}{7}, \frac{24}{7}\right){/tex} : {tex}4 \times \frac{24}{7}+3 \times \frac{24}{7} = {196 \over 7} = 28{/tex} B(0, 6) : {tex}4 \times 0+3 \times 6= 18{/tex} We see that the maximum value of the objective function z is 24 which is at {tex}F\left(5, \frac{4}{3}\right){/tex} and {tex}E\left(\frac{24}{7}, \frac{24}{7}\right){/tex} Thus, the optimal value of Z is 24 As, we know that if an LPP has two optimal solutions, then there are an infinite number of optimal solutions. Therefore, the given objective function can be subjected at an infinite number of points.

If A = [aij]m {tex}\times{/tex} n and B = [aij]m {tex}\times{/tex} p are two matrices, then AB is of order m {tex}\times{/tex} p and is defined as (AB)ik = {tex}\sum_\limits{r=1}^{n}{/tex} air brk = a1kb1k + ai2b2k + ... + ainbnk
Consider A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 2 & -1 \\ 3 & 4 \end{array}\right]{/tex}, B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 5 & 2 \\ 7 & 4 \end{array}\right]{/tex}, C = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 2 & 5 \\ 3 & 8 \end{array}\right]{/tex} and D = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} a & b \\ c & d \end{array}\right]{/tex}


Find the values of a and c in the matrix D such than CD - AB = 0.
questions.explanation: We have, CD - AB = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{ll} 2 & 5 \\ 3 & 8 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll} a & b \\ c & d \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{cc} 3 & 0 \\ 43 & 22 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{cc} 2 a+5 c & 2 b+5 d \\ 3 a+8 c & 3 b+8 d \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{cc} 3 & 0 \\ 43 & 22 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{cc} 2 a+5 c-3 & 2 b+5 d \\ 3 a+8 c-43 & 3 b+8 d-22 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex} By equality of matrices, we get 2a + 5c - 3 = 0 ...(i) 3a + 8c - 43 = 0 ...(ii) 2b + 5d = 0 ...(iii) 3b + 8d - 22 = 0 ...(iv) Solving (i) and (ii), we get a = -191, c = 77
